_meta.purpose: Additive router-strengthening policy for the guided router (limitations §1 signal + §2 tool-calling escalation). This file does NOT replace ruflo-tiers.json (which carries its own schema_version — now 2, an additive/non-breaking change — and is consumed as the per-tier alts map); it is a human-facing REFERENCE POLICY you translate into ruflo env/config so the router scores DIFFICULTY, not length, and treats agentic tool-calling as a hard escalation independent of prompt length. See docs/guide/reference/tiers-and-routing.md (§1/§2) and docs/guide/reference/limitations-and-mitigations.md.
_meta.note: Values are starting points — re-tune against your own Prometheus actuals (docs/guide/reference/observability.md). Tier names are the gateway aliases (tier-fast/tier-heavy/tier-frontier/tier-private).

signal.comment: §1 — length ≠ difficulty. Turn on the shipped neural router instead of the lexical path, and feed it features that correlate with difficulty rather than token count.

neural_router.semantic_route_cache_note: Reuses ruvector-router-core HNSW so near-duplicate prompts skip re-scoring (§1).

escalation.comment: §2 — small local models are specifically weak at agentic tool-calling / multi-turn orchestration, and those turns are NOT necessarily long, so a length-based router cannot see them. Treat these as HARD escalation signals independent of prompt length, and give tool-driven agent types a per-agent-type tier floor.
escalation.uncertainty_escalation_note: Neural-router ensemble disagreement above this threshold forces an up-tier — a quality-floor-beats-quota escalation that bypasses cost bias.

tier_floor_by_agent_type._comment: The router may score DOWN to a cheaper tier, but never BELOW the floor for that agent type. Tool-driven / multi-turn agents start at tier-heavy or tier-frontier regardless of how short the turn looks.
